comment_text.sn22.80:1.2: Elsewhere, the reason is that the monks are too noisy (MN 67:4.6, Ud 3.3:4.1).
comment_text.sn22.80:2.2: Pabāḷha (“purged”) is unique in this context. Compare Sanskrit pravāhana, “driving out, evacuation”.
comment_text.sn22.80:6.1: The unusual use of anta here is, I believe, a play on words. Per the commentary, it suggests the “least” of lifestyles, but it is also that which “makes an end” of suffering and future lives, as said just a few sentences below.
comment_text.sn22.80:6.3: At Pvr 14:3.1, someone who is atthavasika is acting from the earnest intention for a god outcome.
comment_text.sn22.80:6.4: The terms here follow the same sequence as in the Vinaya account of ordination. Generally, ordination should not be given in such cases, but if it is given, those performing it incur an offense of wrong-doing. | Soldiers joined the Sangha to escape military service, so the Buddha said one should not ordain those in service to a king (Kd 1:40.1.1). | Several rules regarding ordination of criminals were passed, the thrust of which is that wanted outlaws should not be ordained (Kd 1:41.1.1). | Another man ordained to escape debt (Kd 1:46.1.1). | Seventeen boys ordained as novices to get a nice livelihood (Kd 1:49.1.1). | The only item that does not straightforwardly correspond is “in fear” (bhayaṭṭa). In the Vinaya sequence, the ordination of slaves appears at this point (Kd 1:47.1.1). The connection is suggested by the verses of the water-carrier Puṇṇikā, who speaks of living “in fear” of her masters’ abuse and beatings (Thig 12.1:1.1).
comment_text.sn22.80:8.4: The “signless” (animitta) meditation is described in the canon simply as, “not focusing on any signs, one enters and remains in the signless immersion of the heart” (SN 41.7:5.2, MN 43:27.2). The current passage distinguishes it from the four satipaṭṭhānas, which are called the “bases of immersion”, namely, the things on which you focus in order to achieve absorption (samādhinimitta, MN 44:12.3, cf. AN 4.14:4.2). Apparently the “signless” (or “baseless” or “featureless”) meditation proceeds without any such meditation topic. At its deepest level, it focuses on the absence of the signs of greed, hatred, and delusion (SN 41.7:6.10). Compare the “undirected” meditation of SN 47.10.
comment_text.sn22.80:9.10: Here the text integrates the aggregates with dependent origination. The aggregates are grasped by way of desire, but they also form the basis of theories of self, which is central to dependent origination (see SN 12.2:6.3 and note).

root_text.sn22.80:1.1: Ekaṁ samayaṁ bhagavā sakkesu viharati kapilavatthusmiṁ nigrodhārāme.
root_text.sn22.80:1.2: Atha kho bhagavā kismiñcideva pakaraṇe bhikkhusaṅghaṁ paṇāmetvā pubbaṇhasamayaṁ nivāsetvā pattacīvaramādāya kapilavatthuṁ piṇḍāya pāvisi.
root_text.sn22.80:1.3: Kapilavatthusmiṁ piṇḍāya caritvā pacchābhattaṁ piṇḍapātapaṭikkanto yena mahāvanaṁ tenupasaṅkami divāvihārāya.
root_text.sn22.80:1.4: Mahāvanaṁ ajjhogāhetvā beluvalaṭṭhikāya mūle divāvihāraṁ nisīdi.
root_text.sn22.80:2.1: Atha kho bhagavato rahogatassa paṭisallīnassa evaṁ cetaso parivitakko udapādi:
root_text.sn22.80:2.3: Santettha bhikkhū navā acirapabbajitā adhunāgatā imaṁ dhammavinayaṁ.
root_text.sn22.80:2.4: Tesaṁ mamaṁ apassantānaṁ siyā aññathattaṁ siyā vipariṇāmo.
root_text.sn22.80:2.5: Seyyathāpi nāma vacchassa taruṇassa mātaraṁ apassantassa siyā aññathattaṁ siyā vipariṇāmo;
root_text.sn22.80:2.6: evameva santettha bhikkhū navā acirapabbajitā adhunāgatā imaṁ dhammavinayaṁ tesaṁ mamaṁ apassantānaṁ siyā aññathattaṁ siyā vipariṇāmo.
root_text.sn22.80:2.7: Seyyathāpi nāma bījānaṁ taruṇānaṁ udakaṁ alabhantānaṁ siyā aññathattaṁ siyā vipariṇāmo;
root_text.sn22.80:2.9: tesaṁ mamaṁ alabhantānaṁ dassanāya siyā aññathattaṁ siyā vipariṇāmo.
root_text.sn22.80:2.10: Yannūnāhaṁ yatheva mayā pubbe bhikkhusaṅgho anuggahito, evameva etarahi anuggaṇheyyaṁ bhikkhusaṅghan”ti.
root_text.sn22.80:3.1: Atha kho brahmā sahampati bhagavato cetasā cetoparivitakkamaññāya—seyyathāpi nāma balavā puriso samiñjitaṁ vā bāhaṁ pasāreyya pasāritaṁ vā bāhaṁ samiñjeyya; evameva—brahmaloke antarahito bhagavato purato pāturahosi.
root_text.sn22.80:3.2: Atha kho brahmā sahampati ekaṁsaṁ uttarāsaṅgaṁ karitvā yena bhagavā tenañjaliṁ paṇāmetvā bhagavantaṁ etadavoca:
root_text.sn22.80:3.5: Santettha bhikkhū navā acirapabbajitā adhunāgatā imaṁ dhammavinayaṁ.
root_text.sn22.80:3.6: Tesaṁ bhagavantaṁ apassantānaṁ siyā aññathattaṁ siyā vipariṇāmo.
root_text.sn22.80:3.7: Seyyathāpi nāma vacchassa taruṇassa mātaraṁ apassantassa siyā aññathattaṁ siyā vipariṇāmo;
root_text.sn22.80:3.8: evameva santettha bhikkhū navā acirapabbajitā adhunāgatā imaṁ dhammavinayaṁ tesaṁ bhagavantaṁ apassantānaṁ siyā aññathattaṁ siyā vipariṇāmo.
root_text.sn22.80:3.9: Seyyathāpi nāma bījānaṁ taruṇānaṁ udakaṁ alabhantānaṁ siyā aññathattaṁ siyā vipariṇāmo;
root_text.sn22.80:3.10: evameva santettha bhikkhū navā acirapabbajitā adhunāgatā imaṁ dhammavinayaṁ, tesaṁ bhagavantaṁ alabhantānaṁ dassanāya siyā aññathattaṁ siyā vipariṇāmo.
root_text.sn22.80:3.13: Yatheva bhagavatā pubbe bhikkhusaṅgho anuggahito, evameva etarahi anuggaṇhātu bhikkhusaṅghan”ti.
root_text.sn22.80:4.2: Atha kho brahmā sahampati bhagavato adhivāsanaṁ viditvā bhagavantaṁ abhivādetvā padakkhiṇaṁ katvā tatthevantaradhāyi.
root_text.sn22.80:5.1: Atha kho bhagavā sāyanhasamayaṁ paṭisallānā vuṭṭhito yena nigrodhārāmo tenupasaṅkami; upasaṅkamitvā paññatte āsane nisīdi.
root_text.sn22.80:5.2: Nisajja kho bhagavā tathārūpaṁ iddhābhisaṅkhāraṁ abhisaṅkhāsi yathā te bhikkhū ekadvīhikāya sārajjamānarūpā yenāhaṁ tenupasaṅkameyyuṁ.
root_text.sn22.80:5.3: Tepi bhikkhū ekadvīhikāya sārajjamānarūpā yena bhagavā tenupasaṅkamiṁsu; upasaṅkamitvā bhagavantaṁ abhivādetvā ekamantaṁ nisīdiṁsu. Ekamantaṁ nisinne kho te bhikkhū bhagavā etadavoca:
root_text.sn22.80:6.2: Abhisāpoyaṁ, bhikkhave, lokasmiṁ piṇḍolo vicarasi pattapāṇīti.
root_text.sn22.80:6.3: Tañca kho etaṁ, bhikkhave, kulaputtā upenti atthavasikā, atthavasaṁ paṭicca;
root_text.sn22.80:6.4: neva rājābhinītā, na corābhinītā, na iṇaṭṭā, na bhayaṭṭā, na ājīvikāpakatā;
root_text.sn22.80:6.5: api ca kho otiṇṇāmha jātiyā jarāya maraṇena sokehi paridevehi dukkhehi domanassehi upāyāsehi dukkhotiṇṇā dukkhaparetā
root_text.sn22.80:6.6: appeva nāma imassa kevalassa dukkhakkhandhassa antakiriyā paññāyethāti.
root_text.sn22.80:7.2: So ca hoti abhijjhālu kāmesu tibbasārāgo byāpannacitto paduṭṭhamanasaṅkappo muṭṭhassati asampajāno asamāhito vibbhantacitto pākatindriyo.
root_text.sn22.80:7.3: Seyyathāpi, bhikkhave, chavālātaṁ ubhatopadittaṁ majjhe gūthagataṁ, neva gāme kaṭṭhatthaṁ pharati, nāraññe kaṭṭhatthaṁ pharati.
root_text.sn22.80:7.4: Tathūpamāhaṁ, bhikkhave, imaṁ puggalaṁ vadāmi gihibhogā ca parihīno, sāmaññatthañca na paripūreti.
root_text.sn22.80:8.3: Ime ca bhikkhave, tayo akusalavitakkā kva aparisesā nirujjhanti?
root_text.sn22.80:8.4: Catūsu vā satipaṭṭhānesu suppatiṭṭhitacittassa viharato animittaṁ vā samādhiṁ bhāvayato.
root_text.sn22.80:8.5: Yāvañcidaṁ, bhikkhave, alameva animitto samādhi bhāvetuṁ.
root_text.sn22.80:8.6: Animitto, bhikkhave, samādhi bhāvito bahulīkato mahapphalo hoti mahānisaṁso.
root_text.sn22.80:9.3: Tatra kho, bhikkhave, sutavā ariyasāvako iti paṭisañcikkhati:
root_text.sn22.80:9.4: ‘atthi nu kho taṁ kiñci lokasmiṁ yamahaṁ upādiyamāno na vajjavā assan’ti?
root_text.sn22.80:9.6: ‘natthi nu kho taṁ kiñci lokasmiṁ yamahaṁ upādiyamāno na vajjavā assaṁ.
root_text.sn22.80:10.4: “Yaṁ panāniccaṁ dukkhaṁ vā taṁ sukhaṁ vā”ti?
root_text.sn22.80:10.6: “Yaṁ panāniccaṁ dukkhaṁ vipariṇāmadhammaṁ kallaṁ nu taṁ samanupassituṁ:

translation_text.sn22.80:1.1: At one time the Buddha was staying in the land of the Sakyans, near Kapilavatthu in the Banyan Tree Monastery.
translation_text.sn22.80:1.2: Then the Buddha, having dismissed the mendicant Saṅgha for some reason, robed up in the morning and, taking his bowl and robe, entered Kapilavatthu for alms.
translation_text.sn22.80:1.3: He wandered for alms in Kapilavatthu. After the meal, on his return from almsround, he went to the Great Wood for the day’s meditation,
translation_text.sn22.80:1.4: plunged deep into it, and sat at the root of a young wood-apple tree to meditate.
translation_text.sn22.80:2.1: Then as he was in private retreat this thought came to his mind,
translation_text.sn22.80:2.3: But there are mendicants here who are junior, recently gone forth, newly come to this teaching and training.
translation_text.sn22.80:2.4: Not seeing me they may change and fall apart.
translation_text.sn22.80:2.5: If a young calf doesn’t see its mother it may change and fall apart. …
translation_text.sn22.80:2.7: Or if young seedlings don’t get water they may change and fall apart.
translation_text.sn22.80:2.8: In the same way, there are mendicants here who are junior, recently gone forth, newly come to this teaching and training.
translation_text.sn22.80:2.9: Not seeing me they may change and fall apart.
translation_text.sn22.80:2.10: Why don’t I support the mendicant Saṅgha now as I did in the past?”
translation_text.sn22.80:3.1: Then the divinity Sahampati knew what the Buddha was thinking. As easily as a strong person would extend or contract their arm, he vanished from the realm of divinity and reappeared in front of the Buddha.
translation_text.sn22.80:3.2: He arranged his robe over one shoulder, raised his joined palms toward the Buddha, and said:
translation_text.sn22.80:3.3: “That’s so true, Blessed One! That’s so true, Holy One!
translation_text.sn22.80:3.4: The Buddha has purged the mendicant Saṅgha.
translation_text.sn22.80:3.5: But there are mendicants who are junior, recently gone forth, newly come to this teaching and training. …
translation_text.sn22.80:3.11: May the Buddha be happy with the mendicant Saṅgha!
translation_text.sn22.80:3.12: May the Buddha welcome the mendicant Saṅgha!
translation_text.sn22.80:3.13: May the Buddha support the mendicant Saṅgha now as he did in the past!”
translation_text.sn22.80:4.2: Then the divinity Sahampati, knowing that the Buddha had consented, bowed, and respectfully circled the Buddha, keeping him on his right, before vanishing right there.
translation_text.sn22.80:5.1: Then in the late afternoon, the Buddha came out of retreat and went to the Banyan Tree Monastery, where he sat on the seat spread out.
translation_text.sn22.80:5.2: Then he used his psychic power to will that the mendicants would come to him timidly, alone or in pairs.
translation_text.sn22.80:5.3: Those mendicants approached the Buddha timidly, bowed, and sat down to one side. The Buddha said to them:
translation_text.sn22.80:6.1: “This is the last of lifestyles, mendicants, namely relying on alms.
translation_text.sn22.80:6.2: The world curses you: ‘You beggar, walking bowl in hand!’
translation_text.sn22.80:6.3: Yet earnest gentlemen take it up for a good reason.
translation_text.sn22.80:6.4: Not to escape a summons by a king or a summons for a bandit, or because you were in debt or in fear, or in order to make a living.
translation_text.sn22.80:6.5: But because they’re swamped by rebirth, old age, and death; by sorrow, lamentation, pain, sadness, and distress. They’re swamped by suffering, mired in suffering.
translation_text.sn22.80:6.6: And they think, ‘Hopefully I can make an end to this entire mass of suffering.’
translation_text.sn22.80:7.1: That’s how this gentleman has gone forth.
translation_text.sn22.80:7.2: Yet they covet sensual pleasures; they’re infatuated, full of ill will and malicious intent. They are unmindful, lacking situational awareness and immersion, with straying mind and undisciplined faculties.
translation_text.sn22.80:7.3: Suppose there was a firebrand for lighting a funeral pyre, burning at both ends, and smeared with dung in the middle. It couldn’t be used as timber either in the village or the wilderness.
translation_text.sn22.80:7.4: I say that individual is just like this. They’ve missed out on the pleasures of the lay life, and haven’t fulfilled the goal of the ascetic life.
translation_text.sn22.80:8.1: There are these three unskillful thoughts.
translation_text.sn22.80:8.3: And where do these three unskillful thoughts cease without anything left over?
translation_text.sn22.80:8.4: In those who meditate with their mind firmly established in the four kinds of mindfulness meditation; or who develop signless immersion.
translation_text.sn22.80:8.5: This is quite enough motivation to develop signless immersion.
translation_text.sn22.80:8.6: When signless immersion is developed and cultivated it is very fruitful and beneficial.
translation_text.sn22.80:9.2: Views favoring existence and views favoring nonexistence.
translation_text.sn22.80:9.3: A learned noble disciple reflects on this:
translation_text.sn22.80:9.4: ‘Is there anything in the world that I could grasp without fault?’
translation_text.sn22.80:9.6: ‘There’s nothing in the world that I could grasp without fault.
translation_text.sn22.80:9.7: For in grasping I would grasp only at form, feeling,
translation_text.sn22.80:9.10: That grasping of mine would be a condition for continued existence.
translation_text.sn22.80:9.11: Continued existence is a requirement for rebirth.
translation_text.sn22.80:9.12: Rebirth is a requirement for old age and death, sorrow, lamentation, pain, sadness, and distress to come to be.
translation_text.sn22.80:9.13: That is how this entire mass of suffering originates.’
translation_text.sn22.80:10.4: “But if it’s impermanent, is it suffering or happiness?”
translation_text.sn22.80:10.6: “But if it’s impermanent, suffering, and perishable, is it fit to be regarded thus:
translation_text.sn22.80:10.7: ‘This is mine, I am this, this is my self’?”
translation_text.sn22.80:10.15: They understand: ‘… there is nothing further for this place.’”

variant_text.sn22.80:5.2: abhisaṅkhāsi → abhisaṅkhāresi (sya-all, km); abhisaṅkhāyi (pts1ed); abhisaṅkharoti (mr) | ekadvīhikāya sārajjamānarūpā yenāhaṁ → etthantare pāṭho sya-all, km potthakesu, yena bhagavā (bj)
